narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 46% chance that you might fail the bar exam on your first try, which is above our threshold for approval. This prediction is based on a careful look at your academic record, test scores, family background, and other details compared to thousands of past students. You have a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.30, which is a bit above the typical 3.21 average, along with middle-family income, full-time study plans, and your background as a white female—details that align closely with many applicants. However, your school decile rankings stand out: in Year 1, you're in the 2nd decile (well below the average 6th), and in Year 3, the 4th decile (slightly under the average 6th), which were the strongest factors pushing the model toward predicting a higher risk of bar failure. Your LSAT score of 31, notably lower than the usual 37, came next in influence, further tipping the scales that way. Family income level, undergraduate GPA, racial background, gender, and full-time status followed in descending order of impact, each nudging the prediction slightly toward failure risk, though less forcefully. Overall, your early and later school performance, combined with the LSAT, carried the most weight in this close-call decision, setting you apart from students who typically pass with stronger rankings and scores. While demographics like yours are common and didn't help or hurt much, they couldn't offset the academic signals. The good news is that several of these key factors are within your control moving forward. Consider retaking the LSAT to aim for a score closer to or above 37, as that's a proven booster for bar success predictions. Strengthening your school deciles through better grades or additional coursework could also help, especially since your UGPA shows you have the potential. In the meantime, exploring bar prep resources or gaining relevant experience might build a stronger profile for your next application—we're rooting for you to turn this around.
